choices.I: CT head to rule out hemorrhage or infarct — INDICATED
choices.J: CT head to rule out hemorrhage or infarct — CONTRAINDICATED
choices.K: Discharge home with oral antihypertensives today — INDICATED
choices.L: Discharge home with oral antihypertensives today — CONTRAINDICATED

matrix.intervention: Oral or sublingual nifedipine for rapid BP reduction
MAP reduction >40% within the first hour
Foley catheter for strict I&O monitoring
CT head to rule out hemorrhage or infarct
Discharge home with oral antihypertensives today

questions.rationale: INDICATED — IV nicardipine/labetalol (A): Titratable IV agents are the standard of care; allow precise, controlled MAP reduction. CONTRAINDICATED — Sublingual nifedipine (D): Produces rapid, unpredictable BP drops; guideline-contraindicated in hypertensive emergency due to ischemia risk. CONTRAINDICATED — MAP >40% reduction in 1 hour (F): Exceeds the safe 25% threshold; risks cerebral, myocardial, and renal ischemia due to impaired autoregulation. INDICATED — Foley catheter (G): Renal perfusion monitoring via strict I&O is critical given creatinine 2.8 and proteinuria indicating nephropathy. INDICATED — CT head (I): Although already completed and negative, CT to rule out hemorrhage/infarct is entirely appropriate and indicated at presentation — guides treatment decisions (e.g., thrombolytics contraindicated if hemorrhage). CONTRAINDICATED — Discharge with oral meds (L): Patient requires ICU-level monitoring, IV titration, and serial organ function assessment; premature discharge risks rebound emergency and death.

title: Acute Severe Pancreatitis: Management and Complications
scenario: A 46-year-old male with a history of heavy alcohol use (12+ drinks/day) is brought to the emergency department by his partner after developing sudden-onset severe epigastric pain rated 9/10, radiating directly through to his back. He reports nausea and has vomited eight times over the past eight hours. He denies recent trauma, new medications, or prior episodes. On assessment, the patient is diaphoretic and appears acutely ill. Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, Temperature 38.8°C, SpO2 94% on room air. Abdomen is diffusely tender with guarding; bowel sounds are diminished. Laboratory results reveal lipase 4,820 U/L (normal <60), amylase 1,240 U/L, WBC 18,600/mm³, hematocrit 52% (hemoconcentration), BUN 34 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, calcium 7.2 mg/dL, and glucose 318 mg/dL. Chest X-ray demonstrates a left pleural effusion. CT abdomen confirms pancreatic inflammation with peripancreatic fat stranding and no evidence of necrosis. RANSON score is calculated at 4, indicating severe acute pancreatitis. The patient is admitted to the ICU for close monitoring and aggressive management.

questions.question: The nurse is planning care for a patient admitted with severe acute pancreatitis (RANSON score 4). Which interventions are indicated? Select all that apply.

choices.A: Initiate aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with Lactated Ringer's solution at 250–500 mL/hr
choices.B: Maintain NPO (nothing by mouth) status to rest the pancreas
choices.C: Administer IV opioid analgesics (e.g., hydromorphone or fentanyl) for pain management
choices.D: Monitor for systemic complications including ARDS, acute kidney injury, and hypocalcemia
choices.E: Insert nasogastric tube routinely to decompress the stomach and prevent aspiration
choices.F: Provide alcohol cessation counseling and refer to addiction services
choices.G: Begin clear liquid diet within 2 hours of admission to prevent gut atrophy
choices.H: Initiate broad-spectrum prophylactic antibiotics to prevent pancreatic infection
choices.I: Administer morphine IV specifically, as it is the preferred opioid for pancreatitis
choices.J: Arrange immediate ERCP within 4 hours regardless of etiology

questions.rationale: Severe acute pancreatitis management centers on aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (250–500 mL/hr Lactated Ringer's preferred over normal saline, as LR reduces systemic inflammation and lowers risk of SIRS); NPO status to minimize pancreatic stimulation; IV opioid analgesia (hydromorphone or fentanyl — NOT morphine specifically, as morphine was historically thought to cause Sphincter of Oddi spasm, though evidence is now mixed); vigilant monitoring for ARDS, AKI, and hypocalcemia as systemic complications; and alcohol cessation counseling since alcohol is the causative factor. NG tube insertion is only indicated for uncontrolled vomiting or gastric outlet obstruction — not routinely. Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended by current guidelines (ACG 2013, IAP 2015) unless infection is confirmed or strongly suspected. ERCP is only indicated within 24–72 hours for gallstone pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis — not this alcohol-related case. Morphine is not specifically indicated and has historically been avoided. Early oral feeding is contraindicated.

questions.question: Complete the clinical reasoning bow-tie for this patient. Identify the most likely condition, select two priority nursing actions, and identify two parameters to monitor.

action_options.A: Initiate aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with Lactated Ringer's and maintain NPO status
action_options.B: Administer IV opioid analgesia and monitor pain response hourly
action_options.D: Administer IV insulin drip for glucose control
action_options.E: Place arterial line and begin vasopressor support immediately
action_options.F: Monitor serum calcium and glucose every 4–6 hours

parameter_options.A: Urine output (goal ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr) to assess fluid resuscitation adequacy and renal perfusion
parameter_options.B: BUN and creatinine trend to detect acute kidney injury
parameter_options.C: Serum calcium level to detect hypocalcemia and prevent tetany/cardiac arrhythmia
parameter_options.D: Respiratory status and SpO2 to detect ARDS development
parameter_options.E: Serum glucose to guide insulin therapy
parameter_options.F: Hematocrit trend to assess hemoconcentration and fluid resuscitation response

questions.rationale: The clinical picture — alcohol use history, sudden severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, lipase >3× normal (4,820 U/L), elevated amylase, hemoconcentration, hypotension, tachycardia, fever, left pleural effusion, and CT findings — is classic for severe acute pancreatitis (RANSON score 4). Perforated peptic ulcer would present with sudden board-like rigidity and free air on CXR. Mesenteric ischemia typically presents in older patients with cardiovascular disease and pain out of proportion to exam. Priority nursing actions are aggressive IVF with LR (addresses hypovolemia and hemoconcentration) and IV opioid analgesia with monitoring. Key parameters include urine output (best bedside indicator of fluid resuscitation adequacy and renal perfusion) and serum calcium (hypocalcemia is a hallmark complication of pancreatitis — saponification of fat consumes calcium). Additional important parameters include BUN/Cr trend, SpO2/respiratory status, glucose, and hematocrit.

questions.question:
Six hours after admission, the nurse reviews the following trending data. The patient has received aggressive IV fluid resuscitation. Which interpretation is MOST accurate?

Trending Data:
• Urine output: 18 mL/hr (goal ≥30 mL/hr)
• Hematocrit: 52% → 48% → 44%
• BUN: 34 → 38 → 42 mg/dL
• Creatinine: 1.8 → 2.1 → 2.6 mg/dL
• Calcium: 7.2 → 6.8 → 6.4 mg/dL
• SpO2: 94% → 91% → 88% (on 2L NC)
• Patient reports increasing dyspnea

choices.A: The patient is responding well to fluid resuscitation; hematocrit is trending down indicating hemodilution
choices.B: The patient is developing acute kidney injury, hypocalcemia, and ARDS as systemic complications of severe pancreatitis
choices.C: The rising BUN and creatinine indicate the patient needs a nephrology consult only; other trends are acceptable
choices.D: The falling SpO2 is expected with a left pleural effusion and does not require immediate intervention
choices.E: The falling calcium is a normal response to IV fluid dilution and does not require treatment
choices.F: The patient's worsening trends indicate pancreatic necrosis has developed and emergency surgery is required

questions.rationale: The trending data reveals three concurrent systemic complications of severe acute pancreatitis developing simultaneously: (1) Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) — urine output persistently below goal at 18 mL/hr despite aggressive IVF, rising BUN (34→42) and creatinine (1.8→2.6) indicating worsening renal function from hypovolemia and inflammatory cytokine-mediated renal injury; (2) Hypocalcemia — calcium falling from 7.2→6.4 mg/dL due to saponification (pancreatic lipase breaks down peripancreatic fat, releasing fatty acids that bind calcium) and hypoalbuminemia — requires urgent intervention as Ca <7.0 risks tetany and cardiac arrhythmias; (3) ARDS — SpO2 falling from 94→88% on supplemental oxygen despite IVF resuscitation indicates developing non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from systemic inflammatory response and cytokine release. While hematocrit is declining (good sign of hemodilution from IVF), the overall trend is one of multi-organ dysfunction requiring immediate provider notification and escalation of care.

questions.question: The patient's serum calcium has declined to 6.2 mg/dL. On assessment, the nurse observes carpopedal spasm when inflating the blood pressure cuff, and elicits a positive Chvostek's sign (facial twitch with tapping over cranial nerve VII). Place the following nursing actions in priority order (1 = highest priority, 4 = lowest).

choices.A: Immediately notify the provider of the calcium level (6.2 mg/dL), carpopedal spasm, and positive Chvostek's sign
choices.B: Place the patient on continuous cardiac monitoring to detect QT interval prolongation and arrhythmias
choices.C: Anticipate and prepare IV calcium gluconate 1–2 g IV per provider order
choices.D: Document findings in the medical record and reassess in 1 hour
choices.E: Administer oral calcium carbonate supplements per hospital protocol
choices.F: Restrict fluid intake to prevent further calcium dilution

questions.rationale: Priority sequence: (1) NOTIFY PROVIDER IMMEDIATELY — calcium of 6.2 mg/dL with symptomatic hypocalcemia (carpopedal spasm = Trousseau's sign, positive Chvostek's sign) is a medical emergency. The provider must be informed to obtain orders for IV calcium replacement. (2) CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING — hypocalcemia causes QT interval prolongation which can precipitate life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias including Torsades de Pointes. Monitoring must be established immediately. (3) PREPARE IV CALCIUM GLUCONATE — anticipate the order; have 1–2 g IV calcium gluconate ready for administration (calcium gluconate is preferred over calcium chloride peripherally as it is less caustic to veins). (4) Documentation is ongoing but is never the first priority with a symptomatic emergency. Oral calcium is inappropriate for symptomatic hypocalcemia requiring IV correction. Fluid restriction is not indicated and would worsen renal perfusion. The PRIORITY framework: Assess → Notify → Intervene → Document.

questions.question:
Complete the following clinical reasoning statement by selecting the correct answer for each blank.

"The nurse recognizes that hemoconcentration (Hct 52%) in acute pancreatitis indicates [BLANK 1] and requires [BLANK 2] to prevent [BLANK 3]. The preferred IV fluid choice is [BLANK 4] because [BLANK 5]."

blank_1.A: third-spacing of fluids into the peritoneal and retroperitoneal space

blank_2.A: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation at 250–500 mL/hr
blank_2.B: diuretic therapy to mobilize third-spaced fluid

blank_3.A: acute kidney injury from renal hypoperfusion

blank_5.A: it reduces systemic inflammatory response (SIRS) markers and C-reactive protein compared to normal saline, which may worsen acidosis
blank_5.B: it contains dextrose to treat the concurrent hyperglycemia
blank_5.C: it is hypotonic and redistributes more effectively into the interstitial space
blank_5.D: it is the only isotonic solution approved for ICU use in pancreatitis

questions.rationale: Hemoconcentration (Hct >44% in males) in acute pancreatitis reflects massive third-spacing — inflammatory mediators (bradykinin, histamine, prostaglandins) increase vascular permeability, causing protein-rich fluid to leak into the peritoneum, retroperitoneum, and peripancreatic tissue. This intravascular volume depletion (despite total body fluid excess) must be corrected with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (250–500 mL/hr initially, titrated to urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr). Failure to adequately resuscitate leads to renal hypoperfusion and acute kidney injury (AKI) — one of the most common and serious complications of severe pancreatitis. Lactated Ringer's (LR) is the preferred fluid based on prospective studies (Wu et al., 2011; Choosakul et al.) showing LR reduces SIRS criteria, CRP levels, and length of stay compared to normal saline. Normal saline in large volumes causes hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, which worsens the inflammatory state. D5W is contraindicated (worsens hyperglycemia). Albumin is not first-line.

questions.question: For each intervention listed, classify it as Indicated, Contraindicated, or Non-essential for the management of this patient with severe acute pancreatitis (alcohol-related, RANSON score 4, no biliary involvement, no confirmed infection).

interventions.A: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with Lactated Ringer's solution (250–500 mL/hr)
interventions.B: Begin immediate solid foods to prevent intestinal atrophy and maintain gut barrier
interventions.C: Administer IV calcium gluconate for serum calcium of 6.2 mg/dL with symptomatic tetany
interventions.D: Initiate prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., imipenem) to prevent infected necrosis
interventions.E: Insert nasojejunal tube and begin enteral nutrition if NPO status exceeds 72 hours
interventions.F: Perform ERCP within 24 hours to evaluate the biliary system

questions.rationale: INDICATED — (A) Aggressive LR resuscitation: cornerstone of severe pancreatitis management; corrects hypovolemia, hemoconcentration, and prevents AKI. Goal urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr. (C) IV calcium gluconate: symptomatic hypocalcemia (Ca 6.2 mg/dL + carpopedal spasm + positive Chvostek's) requires IV replacement — risk of tetany, seizures, and QT prolongation. (E) Nasojejunal enteral nutrition if NPO >72 hours: current guidelines (ACG, ESPEN) strongly recommend enteral nutrition over TPN when feasible — maintains gut barrier integrity, reduces bacterial translocation, lowers infection risk, and improves outcomes; nasojejunal route bypasses pancreatic stimulation. CONTRAINDICATED — (B) Immediate solid foods: oral intake stimulates pancreatic secretion, worsening inflammation — NPO is mandatory in acute phase; early feeding is not appropriate before clinical improvement. (D) Prophylactic antibiotics: multiple RCTs and meta-analyses have shown no mortality benefit from prophylactic antibiotics in acute pancreatitis (even with necrosis) and increase risk of resistant organisms and fungal infection — only indicated if infection is confirmed (e.g., infected necrosis, cholangitis, bacteremia). NON-ESSENTIAL — (F) ERCP within 24 hours: ERCP is indicated only for gallstone (biliary) pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis or biliary obstruction. This patient has alcohol-related pancreatitis with no biliary involvement — ERCP carries procedural risks without benefit in this context.

title: Pediatric Bacterial Meningitis: Emergency Recognition and Care
scenario: A 6-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his parents with a 12-hour history of high fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. Over the past hour, he has become increasingly lethargic. His parents report he has been immunized but missed his Hib and MenACWY booster doses. Current vital signs: Temperature 40.1°C, Heart Rate 148 bpm, Blood Pressure 88/52 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 28 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Weight: 22 kg. Neurological assessment reveals a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 12 (E3V4M5), positive Kernig's sign, positive Brudzinski's sign, and photophobia. On skin assessment, the nurse identifies 3 petechial lesions on the trunk and extremities that appear to be spreading. Two sets of blood cultures have been drawn. A lumbar puncture (LP) has been ordered; results are pending. The child is placed in a room immediately. The nurse recognizes this presentation as a potential life-threatening neurological emergency requiring rapid assessment, isolation precautions, and time-sensitive interventions. The clinical team is coordinating emergent diagnostics and empiric treatment.

questions.question: The nurse is caring for the 6-year-old with suspected bacterial meningitis. LP results are still pending. Select ALL actions that are IMMEDIATE nursing and clinical priorities at this time. Select all that apply.

choices.A: Initiate empiric IV antibiotics immediately — do NOT wait for LP results if LP is delayed or patient is deteriorating
choices.B: Place the patient in droplet isolation immediately
choices.C: Establish IV access and begin fluid resuscitation for hypotension (BP 88/52)
choices.D: Perform frequent neurological checks every 15–30 minutes to monitor GCS trend
choices.E: Dim the lights in the patient's room to reduce photophobia and ICP stimulation
choices.F: Administer dexamethasone before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce inflammatory sequelae
choices.G: Hold antibiotics and wait for LP results before starting any treatment
choices.H: Place the patient in airborne isolation with N95 precautions
choices.I: Restrict IV fluids to avoid worsening cerebral edema

questions.rationale: In suspected bacterial meningitis, empiric IV antibiotics (typically ceftriaxone) must be initiated immediately — delay increases mortality and neurological morbidity. Antibiotics should NOT be withheld while awaiting LP results if there is any delay or clinical deterioration. Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces inflammation and risk of hearing loss. Droplet isolation is required for suspected Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcal disease). IV access and fluid resuscitation are urgent given the BP of 88/52 (hypotensive for a 6-year-old). Frequent neuro checks detect deterioration early. Dimming lights reduces photophobia and limits unnecessary stimulation. Airborne isolation (H) is INCORRECT — meningococcal meningitis is droplet-transmitted, not airborne. Waiting for LP before antibiotics (G) and restricting fluids (I) are dangerous and contraindicated in this hemodynamically unstable patient.

questions.question:
Complete the clinical reasoning bow-tie for this patient. The nurse must identify the most likely condition, select appropriate nursing actions, and identify parameters to monitor. For each column, select the best options.

COLUMN 1 — Most likely condition based on current findings:
COLUMN 2 — Nursing actions to take NOW:
COLUMN 3 — Parameters to monitor closely:

choices.A: [CONDITION] Bacterial meningitis with early meningococcemia
choices.B: [CONDITION] Viral (aseptic) meningitis
choices.C: [CONDITION] Isolated meningococcal septicemia without meningitis
choices.D: [ACTION] Administer empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone) plus dexamethasone as ordered
choices.E: [ACTION] Implement droplet precautions and cohort isolation
choices.F: [ACTION] Initiate IV fluid resuscitation for septic shock (20 mL/kg NS bolus)
choices.G: [ACTION] Apply oxygen and prepare for possible intubation if GCS continues to fall
choices.H: [PARAMETER] GCS trend — declining GCS indicates worsening ICP or septic progression
choices.I: [PARAMETER] Rash progression — petechiae spreading or becoming purpuric indicates fulminant meningococcemia
choices.J: [PARAMETER] Blood pressure trend — worsening hypotension indicates septic shock
choices.K: [PARAMETER] Fontanelle assessment in infants and signs of increased ICP (papilledema, Cushing's triad)

questions.rationale: This child's presentation — fever, meningeal signs (Kernig's, Brudzinski's), altered mental status (GCS 12), petechial rash, hypotension, and missed MenACWY vaccine — is classic for bacterial meningitis with early meningococcemia. Viral meningitis (B) does not typically cause petechiae, hemodynamic instability, or this degree of acute deterioration. Isolated septicemia (C) can occur but the presence of neck stiffness and meningeal signs points to meningeal involvement. Immediate actions include empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone (D), droplet precautions (E), fluid resuscitation (F), and airway readiness (G). Key monitoring parameters are GCS trend (H), rash progression — particularly petechiae becoming purpuric, which signals fulminant meningococcemia (I), blood pressure trend for septic shock (J), and signs of increased ICP including fontanelle changes in infants (K).

questions.question:
The nurse reviews the patient's trending assessment data over the past 2 hours:

| Time  | Petechiae         | GCS | BP      |
|-------|-------------------|-----|---------|
| 0800  | 3 lesions, trunk  | 12  | 88/52   |
| 0900  | 8 lesions, trunk/legs | 11 | 82/48 |
| 1000  | 15 lesions, 2 becoming purpuric | 9 | 74/44 |

Based on these trends, what does this clinical picture indicate and what is the PRIORITY nursing response?

choices.A: This trend indicates improving perfusion; continue monitoring every 2 hours
choices.B: This trend indicates fulminant meningococcemia with septic shock; activate rapid response team, prepare for emergent PICU transfer, initiate vasopressor support, and continue aggressive fluid resuscitation
choices.C: This trend indicates a normal progression of bacterial meningitis; administer acetaminophen for fever and reassess in 1 hour
choices.D: This trend indicates viral hemorrhagic fever; implement airborne precautions and notify infection control
choices.E: This trend indicates a drug reaction to antibiotics; discontinue current antibiotics and switch agents
choices.F: This trend indicates dehydration from fever; increase oral fluid intake and monitor urine output

questions.rationale: This trend is a medical emergency. The progression of petechiae (3 → 8 → 15 lesions), conversion of lesions to purpura (non-blanching hemorrhagic skin necrosis), deteriorating GCS (12 → 11 → 9), and worsening hypotension (88/52 → 74/44) over 2 hours is the hallmark of fulminant meningococcemia with septic shock and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). This carries an extremely high mortality without immediate escalation. Priority actions include: activating the rapid response team, preparing for emergent PICU transfer, initiating vasopressor therapy (dopamine or norepinephrine), continuing aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (repeat 20 mL/kg boluses), ensuring antibiotics are infusing, drawing stat coagulation studies (PT/PTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer for DIC), and preparing for possible intubation given GCS of 9. A GCS ≤8 typically meets criteria for airway protection. This is NOT a normal progression (A), NOT a drug reaction (E), and NOT a dehydration issue (F).

questions.question: The child's condition has deteriorated. Current status: GCS 8, BP 74/44 mmHg, petechiae spreading rapidly with 2 lesions now purpuric. The attending provider is at the bedside. Which nursing actions should the nurse prioritize and coordinate simultaneously? Rank the following from highest to most time-critical coordination (select the response that BEST reflects appropriate priority nursing action).

choices.A: Document the assessment findings in the EMR, then notify the charge nurse, then call pharmacy for vasopressor orders
choices.B: Wait for the provider to write all orders before taking any nursing action to avoid medication errors
choices.C: Coordinate simultaneously: ensure antibiotics are infusing, establish a second large-bore IV access, draw stat labs (CBC, CMP, coags, blood gas, lactate), prepare vasopressor infusion (dopamine), and activate PICU transfer — communicate findings using SBAR to the team
choices.D: Place the patient in a Trendelenburg position and administer oral fluids to improve blood pressure
choices.E: Notify the patient's parents about the diagnosis first, then proceed with interventions
choices.F: Reassess vital signs in 15 minutes to confirm the blood pressure reading before escalating care

questions.rationale: In this deteriorating patient with GCS 8 and BP 74/44 (severe septic shock), nursing actions must be simultaneous and coordinated — not sequential. While the provider is present and issuing orders, the nurse should: (1) confirm antibiotics are infusing (highest priority — the medication that treats the cause), (2) establish a second large-bore IV for vasopressor infusion and rapid fluid delivery, (3) draw stat labs including CBC, metabolic panel, coagulation studies, ABG/VBG, and lactate to guide resuscitation and assess for DIC, (4) prepare vasopressor infusion (dopamine is first-line in pediatric septic shock when fluid-refractory), and (5) activate PICU transfer process and communicate using SBAR. Documentation (A) is important but not before life-saving interventions. Waiting for written orders (B) wastes critical time when provider is present and verbal orders are valid. Trendelenburg position (D) is no longer recommended and oral fluids are inappropriate in a GCS 8 patient due to aspiration risk. Family communication (E) is important but not before stabilization. Rechecking BP in 15 minutes (F) is dangerous delay — this child needs immediate action.

questions.question:
Complete the following clinical reasoning statement by selecting the correct term for each blank:

"The nurse recognizes that spreading [BLANK 1] in a febrile child with meningeal signs indicates [BLANK 2] and requires [BLANK 3] BEFORE [BLANK 4] if the patient is [BLANK 5]."

Select the best answer that correctly fills ALL five blanks in order:

choices.A: Blank 1: urticaria | Blank 2: viral meningitis | Blank 3: antiviral therapy | Blank 4: blood cultures | Blank 5: immunocompromised
choices.B: Blank 1: petechiae/purpura | Blank 2: meningococcemia | Blank 3: immediate empiric antibiotics | Blank 4: lumbar puncture | Blank 5: hemodynamically unstable or has signs of increased ICP
choices.C: Blank 1: maculopapular rash | Blank 2: bacterial endocarditis | Blank 3: echocardiogram | Blank 4: antibiotic therapy | Blank 5: febrile
choices.D: Blank 1: petechiae/purpura | Blank 2: idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura | Blank 3: platelet transfusion | Blank 4: bone marrow biopsy | Blank 5: platelet count is below 50,000
choices.E: Blank 1: petechiae | Blank 2: meningococcemia | Blank 3: lumbar puncture | Blank 4: antibiotics | Blank 5: febrile
choices.F: Blank 1: ecchymosis | Blank 2: coagulopathy | Blank 3: FFP transfusion | Blank 4: hematology consult | Blank 5: actively bleeding

questions.rationale: The correct clinical reasoning statement reads: 'The nurse recognizes that spreading PETECHIAE/PURPURA in a febrile child with meningeal signs indicates MENINGOCOCCEMIA and requires IMMEDIATE EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS BEFORE LUMBAR PUNCTURE if the patient is HEMODYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE OR HAS SIGNS OF INCREASED ICP.' Petechiae and purpura (non-blanching lesions) are the hallmark skin findings of meningococcemia — caused by Neisseria meningitidis endotoxin triggering DIC and vascular endothelial damage. While LP is the gold standard for diagnosing meningitis, it must NOT be performed if the patient is hemodynamically unstable or shows signs of increased ICP (risk of brain herniation). Antibiotics must be given immediately in these cases — a 30-minute delay in antibiotics increases mortality significantly. Choice E is a trap — it reverses the correct order (LP before antibiotics), which is contraindicated in unstable patients. ITP (D) can cause petechiae but without meningeal signs, fever of this magnitude, or hemodynamic compromise.

questions.question:
For each nursing or clinical action below, indicate whether it is INDICATED or CONTRAINDICATED in the care of this 6-year-old with suspected bacterial meningitis/meningococcemia.

Select the answer choice that correctly classifies ALL six actions:

choices.A: 1-Indicated, 2-Indicated, 3-Indicated, 4-Indicated, 5-Indicated, 6-Indicated
choices.B: 1-Indicated, 2-Indicated, 3-Contraindicated, 4-Indicated, 5-Contraindicated, 6-Indicated
choices.C: 1-Contraindicated, 2-Indicated, 3-Contraindicated, 4-Indicated, 5-Indicated, 6-Indicated
choices.D: 1-Indicated, 2-Contraindicated, 3-Contraindicated, 4-Contraindicated, 5-Contraindicated, 6-Indicated
choices.E: 1-Indicated, 2-Indicated, 3-Indicated, 4-Contraindicated, 5-Contraindicated, 6-Contraindicated
choices.F: 1-Contraindicated, 2-Contraindicated, 3-Indicated, 4-Indicated, 5-Indicated, 6-Contraindicated

matrix_items.action: Place patient in droplet isolation (surgical mask for HCWs, private room, limit transport)
matrix_items.rationale: Neisseria meningitidis is transmitted via respiratory droplets. Droplet precautions are mandatory for the first 24 hours of antibiotic therapy.

matrix_items.action: Administer empiric antibiotics before lumbar puncture if patient is hemodynamically unstable
matrix_items.rationale: LP should not delay antibiotics in unstable patients. Empiric treatment (ceftriaxone) must begin immediately. A brief antibiotic exposure does not significantly alter CSF culture results within the first few hours.

matrix_items.action: Wait for lumbar puncture results before initiating any antibiotic therapy
matrix_items.rationale: Delaying antibiotics to wait for LP results is dangerous and increases mortality. If LP is delayed for any reason (patient instability, coagulopathy, papilledema, ICP signs), antibiotics must be started immediately.

matrix_items.action: Administer dexamethasone concurrently with or immediately before the first antibiotic dose
matrix_items.rationale: Dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 4 days) reduces meningeal inflammation and significantly decreases the risk of hearing loss in bacterial meningitis, especially H. influenzae type b. It must be given with or just before the first antibiotic dose to be effective.

matrix_items.action: Place patient in airborne isolation with N95 respirators instead of droplet precautions
matrix_items.rationale: Meningococcal meningitis is NOT airborne — it is transmitted via large respiratory droplets (>5 microns) that travel less than 3 feet. Airborne precautions with N95 respirators are used for tuberculosis, measles, and varicella. Using airborne instead of droplet precautions wastes resources and may create confusion about the correct precaution level.

matrix_items.action: Notify public health authorities and identify close contacts for prophylactic antibiotics (rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone)
matrix_items.rationale: Meningococcal disease is a reportable condition. Close contacts (household members, daycare contacts, anyone with prolonged face-to-face exposure) require chemoprophylaxis to prevent secondary cases. Public health notification facilitates contact tracing and community protection.

questions.rationale: Correct classifications: (1) Droplet isolation — INDICATED (meningococcal is droplet-transmitted, required for first 24h of antibiotics). (2) Empiric antibiotics before LP if unstable — INDICATED (never delay antibiotics for LP in an unstable patient). (3) Wait for LP before antibiotics — CONTRAINDICATED (dangerous delay that increases mortality). (4) Dexamethasone with first antibiotic dose — INDICATED (reduces inflammation and hearing loss risk; must be given before or with first dose to be effective). (5) Airborne isolation instead of droplet — CONTRAINDICATED (meningococcal is droplet, not airborne; N95 not required). (6) Notify public health and identify contacts — INDICATED (reportable disease; close contacts need prophylaxis). Answer B correctly classifies all six actions.

title: Post-Cardiac Catheterization: Femoral Access Site Monitoring
scenario: A 62-year-old male is 2 hours post-percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) via right femoral artery access. A drug-eluting stent was successfully placed in the left anterior descending (LAD) artery for a 90% occlusion found on diagnostic angiography. The patient received a heparin drip during the procedure, which was discontinued 30 minutes ago. He was administered aspirin 325mg and ticagrelor 180mg loading dose per post-PCI protocol. Current vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 78 bpm (normal sinus rhythm), RR 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 98.4°F. Assessment of the right groin reveals manual pressure has been applied; a small hematoma measuring 3x3 cm is noted at the puncture site with no active oozing. Right foot is warm to touch, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+, and capillary refill is 2 seconds. Pre-procedure creatinine was 1.1 mg/dL; contrast volume used was 180 mL. The patient reports the right leg 'feels numb and tingly.' Urine output for the last hour is 22 mL. The nurse is responsible for post-procedure monitoring and must assess for potential access site and systemic complications.

questions.question: The nurse is planning post-procedure care for this patient following femoral artery PCI. Select ALL assessments and interventions that are essential in the immediate post-catheterization period. Select all that apply.

choices.A: Assess the access site every 15 minutes for signs of hematoma expansion, active bleeding, or bruising
choices.B: Check distal pulses and complete neurovascular status bilaterally (color, warmth, sensation, movement, capillary refill)
choices.C: Encourage IV fluid hydration to promote diuresis and prevent contrast-induced nephropathy
choices.D: Instruct the patient to ambulate to the hallway immediately to prevent deep vein thrombosis
choices.E: Maintain the right leg straight and immobile per post-catheterization protocol for the required hold time
choices.F: Monitor for signs of retroperitoneal bleeding including new onset flank or back pain, abdominal rigidity, and unexpected hypotension
choices.G: Maintain strict intake and output measurements; report urine output less than 0.5 mL/kg/hr
choices.H: Apply a warm compress to the hematoma site to reduce discomfort and promote reabsorption
choices.I: Hold aspirin and ticagrelor until the access site is fully healed to reduce bleeding risk
choices.J: Obtain a 12-lead ECG and monitor for chest pain, ST changes, or signs of acute stent occlusion

questions.rationale: Post-femoral PCI nursing priorities include: (A) q15min access site checks to catch early hematoma expansion before it becomes life-threatening; (B) bilateral neurovascular checks to detect arterial compromise including loss of pulse, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis, or pain (the 5 P's); (C) IV hydration is the primary prevention for contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), especially with 180 mL contrast and a borderline creatinine of 1.1; (E) leg immobility is required to prevent re-bleeding at the femoral access site — duration depends on closure method; (F) retroperitoneal hemorrhage is a life-threatening, occult complication of femoral access — early signs include flank/back pain and unexplained hemodynamic instability; (G) urine output monitoring detects CIN early; (J) post-stent ECG monitoring detects acute stent thrombosis. INCORRECT: (D) Ambulation is contraindicated immediately post-femoral access — typically 2–6 hours of bed rest is required. (H) Warm compresses promote vasodilation and can worsen bleeding at a fresh hematoma. (I) Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) with aspirin and ticagrelor is mandatory post-PCI to prevent acute stent thrombosis — holding it risks catastrophic in-stent clotting.

questions.question: The nurse is evaluating the patient's right groin finding: a 3x3 cm hematoma at the femoral access site with the patient reporting numbness and tingling in the right leg. Using clinical reasoning, complete the following bow-tie analysis by identifying the most likely condition, the two priority nursing actions, and the two parameters to monitor most closely.

choices.A: Condition: Femoral hematoma — localized blood collection at puncture site, currently stable
choices.B: Condition: Retroperitoneal hemorrhage — occult bleed tracking posterior to the peritoneum
choices.C: Condition: Femoral artery pseudoaneurysm — pulsatile hematoma from incomplete vessel closure
choices.D: Condition: Normal post-procedure finding — hematoma expected, no intervention needed
choices.E: Action: Apply manual pressure to the access site and reassess in 15 minutes
choices.F: Action: Immediately notify the interventional cardiologist of hematoma size and neurovascular symptoms
choices.G: Action: Administer a warm compress and elevate the leg above heart level
choices.H: Action: Perform complete bilateral lower extremity neurovascular checks every 15 minutes
choices.I: Parameter: Hematoma size and rate of expansion over time
choices.J: Parameter: Distal pulse quality and capillary refill in both feet
choices.K: Parameter: Patient's pain score on a 0–10 scale only
choices.L: Parameter: Patient's appetite and oral intake

questions.rationale: CONDITION (A): The current presentation — small 3x3 cm hematoma, hemodynamically stable BP 138/82, intact 2+ distal pulses, warm foot, cap refill 2 sec — is consistent with a localized femoral hematoma, a common and expected post-catheterization finding. However, neurovascular symptoms (numbness/tingling) require close surveillance. Retroperitoneal hemorrhage (B) typically presents with flank/back pain, abdominal pain, and hemodynamic instability — not yet present. Pseudoaneurysm (C) is suspected when a pulsatile mass is present. 'Normal, no intervention needed' (D) is incorrect — a symptomatic hematoma with paresthesias requires active monitoring and may progress. ACTIONS: (E) Manual pressure is the first-line intervention for hematoma control. (H) Bilateral neurovascular checks every 15 minutes are mandatory to detect progressive vascular compromise early. Warm compress (G) is contraindicated. PARAMETERS: (I) Hematoma size is the most direct indicator of expansion. (J) Distal pulses and cap refill are critical for detecting arterial compromise progressing to limb ischemia.

questions.question:
The nurse documents the following trending data over 90 minutes following the initial assessment. Review the trend and select the best interpretation of what is occurring.

| Parameter | T=0 (Initial) | T=30 min | T=90 min |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hematoma size | 3x3 cm | 5x5 cm | 8x7 cm |
| BP | 138/82 mmHg | 124/74 mmHg | 102/60 mmHg |
| HR | 78 bpm | 88 bpm | 106 bpm |
| Right foot cap refill | 2 sec | 3 sec | 5 sec |
| Right foot pulses | 2+ | 1+ | Weak/doppler only |
| Pain (right leg) | Mild | Moderate | Severe |

The patient is now diaphoretic and anxious. What does this trend indicate?

choices.A: The hematoma is resolving normally; the hemodynamic changes reflect post-procedure stress response and are expected
choices.B: The patient is developing contrast-induced nephropathy, evidenced by declining BP and rising HR
choices.C: The patient is experiencing an expanding femoral hematoma with progressive vascular compromise, consistent with inadequate hemostasis or pseudoaneurysm formation, requiring immediate intervention
choices.D: The hemodynamic changes are caused by a vasovagal response to pain; administration of atropine and IV fluid bolus will resolve the trend
choices.E: The patient is experiencing acute in-stent thrombosis of the LAD stent; the leg findings are unrelated
choices.F: The patient has retroperitoneal hemorrhage only; the groin hematoma is a normal finding and the hemodynamic deterioration is from the occult bleed

questions.rationale: This trend demonstrates a classic pattern of expanding femoral hematoma with evolving vascular compromise: (1) Progressive hematoma expansion from 3x3 → 8x7 cm indicates ongoing hemorrhage at the access site — inadequate hemostasis, pseudoaneurysm, or arteriovenous fistula. (2) Declining BP (138/82 → 102/60) and rising HR (78 → 106) indicate hemorrhagic shock — the patient is losing significant blood volume. (3) Deteriorating capillary refill (2 → 5 sec) and weakening distal pulses signal progressive arterial compromise and potential acute limb ischemia. (4) Increasing pain correlates with hematoma expansion compressing surrounding structures and ischemia. This is a CRITICAL emergency requiring immediate interventional cardiologist notification, preparation for surgical or IR vascular intervention (possibly ultrasound-guided thrombin injection for pseudoaneurysm or surgical repair), and resuscitation. INCORRECT: (A) Nothing about this trend is 'normal.' (B) CIN presents with rising creatinine/oliguria, not hemodynamic collapse. (D) Vasovagal responses typically include bradycardia, not tachycardia. (E) Stent thrombosis presents with chest pain/ST elevation. (F) Retroperitoneal hemorrhage is possible concurrently but this trend directly reflects the visible expanding groin hematoma as the primary driver.

questions.question: On reassessment, the nurse finds the following: Right foot capillary refill is now 5 seconds. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are absent bilaterally on right. Right leg is cool, pale, and mottled from the knee down. The patient reports severe, worsening pain rated 9/10 and states his right foot 'feels completely numb.' BP 98/58 mmHg, HR 112 bpm. Prioritize the following nursing actions by placing them in the correct order of priority (1 = highest priority).

choices.A: Apply a warm compress to the right foot to promote circulation and relieve numbness
choices.B: Immediately notify the interventional cardiologist and vascular surgery of suspected acute limb ischemia — this is a limb-threatening emergency
choices.C: Document findings thoroughly in the electronic health record before taking action
choices.D: Prepare for emergency vascular intervention (surgical embolectomy, endovascular repair, or IR-guided thrombin injection) — gather consent, IV access, lab draws (CBC, coag, type and screen)
choices.E: Apply firm manual pressure to the groin hematoma and call a rapid response / activate emergency protocol
choices.F: Administer oral pain medication and reassure the patient that numbness after catheterization is common and will resolve

questions.rationale: PRIORITY 1 — (B): Absent pulses + cool/pale/mottled limb + 5-second cap refill = ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA (ALI). This is a surgical/vascular emergency. The 'golden window' for limb salvage is typically 4–6 hours from onset of ischemia. Immediate physician notification is the single most time-critical action. PRIORITY 2 — (E): While activating emergency response, manual pressure controls ongoing hemorrhage contributing to the hematoma and vascular compromise. Rapid Response activation mobilizes additional resources. PRIORITY 3 — (D): Prepare for emergent intervention — surgical or IR. Type and screen, coag panel, consent, large-bore IV access, and NPO status are all time-sensitive preparatory actions. PRIORITY 4 — (C): Documentation is important but follows life/limb-saving actions. NEVER prioritized over intervention in an emergency. INCORRECT: (A) Warm compresses are CONTRAINDICATED in ischemic limbs — heat increases metabolic demand in tissue that is already oxygen-deprived, worsening ischemic injury. (F) Complete absence of pulses with mottling is NOT a normal finding — reassurance without action is dangerous and a failure of duty.

questions.question:
Complete the following nursing documentation note by selecting the best option for each blank.

'Following femoral artery catheterization with 180 mL of contrast administered, the nurse monitors urine output hourly to detect early signs of [BLANK 1], a complication caused by contrast dye toxicity to the renal tubules. The primary prevention strategy is [BLANK 2]. The patient's current urine output of 22 mL over the past hour is [BLANK 3], which warrants [BLANK 4] to achieve a target urine output of [BLANK 5].'

BLANK_2.A: administration of furosemide to force diuresis
BLANK_2.B: holding all nephrotoxic medications for 72 hours
BLANK_2.C: adequate IV fluid hydration before and after the procedure using isotonic crystalloid

BLANK_3.A: within normal limits for a post-procedure patient
BLANK_3.B: below the acceptable threshold and consistent with oliguria
BLANK_3.D: expected given the patient's age and baseline creatinine

BLANK_4.A: reassessment in 2 hours with no immediate action required
BLANK_4.B: oral encouragement of fluids and dietary sodium restriction
BLANK_4.C: notifying the provider and increasing the IV fluid rate per protocol
BLANK_4.D: administration of furosemide 40 mg IV to stimulate output

BLANK_5.A: at least 30 mL/hr regardless of patient weight
BLANK_5.D: equal to intake to maintain strict euvolemia

questions.rationale: BLANK 1 — (B) Contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN): Iodinated contrast dye causes direct renal tubular toxicity and renal vasoconstriction. Risk is elevated with large contrast volumes (>100–150 mL), pre-existing CKD, diabetes, and poor hydration. This patient used 180 mL with baseline Cr 1.1 — borderline elevated risk. BLANK 2 — (C) Adequate IV hydration with isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl or LR) is the gold standard and most evidence-based prevention for CIN. It dilutes contrast, maintains renal perfusion, and promotes diuresis. Furosemide is NOT recommended prophylactically — it can worsen dehydration and concentrate contrast in tubules. BLANK 3 — (B) 22 mL/hr is oliguria. Normal urine output is ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr (approximately 30–50 mL/hr for most adults). At 22 mL/hr, this patient is below the acceptable threshold and at risk for CIN progression. BLANK 4 — (C) The appropriate action is provider notification and IV fluid rate increase. Do not administer furosemide to a patient with oliguria post-contrast — this worsens volume depletion. BLANK 5 — (B) The clinical standard for adequate renal perfusion is ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr. This is weight-based and the most pharmacologically accurate threshold used in critical care and post-procedure nursing.

questions.question: The nurse is reviewing post-PCI care orders and nursing interventions. For each intervention listed, classify it as Indicated (appropriate and should be implemented), Contraindicated (harmful and should NOT be implemented), or Not Applicable (irrelevant to this clinical situation).

choices.A: Perform bilateral lower extremity neurovascular checks (pulses, cap refill, color, sensation, movement) every 15 minutes for the first 2 hours post-procedure
choices.B: Encourage the patient to ambulate to the hallway within 1 hour of femoral artery catheterization to prevent DVT
choices.C: Administer IV isotonic crystalloid fluid bolus/increased rate to maintain urine output and prevent contrast-induced nephropathy
choices.D: Apply a warm compress to the right groin hematoma to reduce pain and promote reabsorption
choices.E: Hold aspirin 325mg and ticagrelor for 48–72 hours post-PCI to minimize access site bleeding risk
choices.F: Apply continuous firm manual pressure to an expanding femoral hematoma and notify the interventional cardiologist immediately

questions.rationale: A — INDICATED: Bilateral neurovascular checks every 15 minutes are the standard of care post-femoral catheterization. Early detection of pulse loss, prolonged cap refill, pallor, or new paresthesias allows for timely intervention before irreversible limb ischemia occurs. B — CONTRAINDICATED: Femoral artery catheterization requires a period of strict bed rest with the affected leg straight and immobile — typically 2–6 hours depending on closure device used (manual compression requires longer hold). Premature ambulation risks re-opening the arteriotomy site, causing hemorrhage or hematoma expansion. DVT prophylaxis is addressed through other means post-PCI. C — INDICATED: IV hydration is the primary evidence-based intervention to prevent contrast-induced nephropathy. With 180 mL of contrast, borderline creatinine, and oliguria at 22 mL/hr, increasing IV fluid rate is both appropriate and urgent. D — CONTRAINDICATED: Warm compresses cause local vasodilation and increased blood flow, which can actively expand an existing hematoma and promote further bleeding at the femoral puncture site. Cold compresses (if ordered) or continued manual pressure are preferred. E — CONTRAINDICATED: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) — aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor) — is absolutely required post-coronary stenting. Holding DAPT significantly increases the risk of acute in-stent thrombosis, which carries up to 40–50% mortality. The risk of stent thrombosis far outweighs the marginal benefit of reduced access site bleeding. F — INDICATED: Manual pressure is the first-line intervention for an expanding femoral hematoma. Simultaneous notification of the interventional cardiologist is essential, as expansion may indicate pseudoaneurysm formation, AV fistula, or inadequate vessel closure requiring vascular intervention.

title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome: Delirium Tremens Management
scenario: A 48-year-old male was admitted 36 hours ago for community-acquired pneumonia and is currently receiving ceftriaxone. Social history reveals a 10-year history of heavy alcohol use, consuming approximately 1 liter of vodka daily. His last drink was on the day of admission. Over the past several hours, the patient has become increasingly agitated, diaphoretic, and tremulous. He is now hallucinating, repeatedly pointing at the walls and shouting about insects crawling on them. Current vital signs: BP 168/104 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, Temp 38.6°C, SpO2 96% on room air. Neurological assessment reveals nystagmus and bilaterally dilated pupils. The Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) score is 28, indicating severe withdrawal. Twenty minutes ago, the patient experienced a new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure that self-resolved after 90 seconds. Current medications include ceftriaxone for pneumonia and thiamine 100 mg IV daily (started on admission). Lorazepam 2 mg IV q6h PRN has been ordered; the last dose was administered 5 hours ago. The nurse recognizes this clinical picture as delirium tremens and initiates the alcohol withdrawal protocol.

questions.question: The nurse is caring for the patient described in the scenario who has a CIWA-Ar score of 28 and experienced a tonic-clonic seizure 20 minutes ago. Select ALL interventions that are indicated at this time. Select all that apply.

choices.A: Administer IV lorazepam immediately per the CIWA-Ar protocol, as a score of 28 indicates severe withdrawal requiring urgent pharmacologic intervention
choices.B: Implement seizure precautions: pad side rails, lower the bed to the lowest position, and ensure the call light is within reach
choices.C: Administer thiamine BEFORE any glucose-containing fluids or dextrose to prevent precipitation of Wernicke's encephalopathy
choices.D: Place the patient in a quiet, low-stimulation environment; dim lights, reduce noise, and limit unnecessary visitors
choices.E: Apply physical restraints immediately as the first-line intervention to manage agitation and prevent self-harm
choices.F: Withhold benzodiazepines due to recent seizure activity and concern for respiratory depression; use haloperidol instead

questions.rationale: A) CORRECT: A CIWA-Ar score of 28 is severe (>15 = severe withdrawal). With a recent seizure, IV benzodiazepine administration is urgently indicated. Delaying treatment risks status epilepticus and death. B) CORRECT: Post-seizure, seizure precautions are mandatory. Padded side rails prevent injury if another seizure occurs; a lowered bed and accessible call light are standard safety measures. C) CORRECT: In chronic alcohol users, glucose administration before thiamine can precipitate Wernicke's encephalopathy (Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome) by depleting thiamine needed for glucose metabolism. Thiamine MUST be given first. D) CORRECT: Sensory stimulation exacerbates agitation, hallucinations, and autonomic instability in DTs. A calm, quiet, dimly lit environment is a key non-pharmacologic intervention. E) INCORRECT: Physical restraints are NOT the first-line intervention for agitation in alcohol withdrawal. Restraints can increase agitation, worsen hyperthermia, and do not address the underlying withdrawal. Benzodiazepines are the appropriate first-line treatment. F) INCORRECT: Withholding benzodiazepines is dangerous and contraindicated. Benzodiazepines are the FIRST-LINE treatment for alcohol withdrawal seizures and DTs. Haloperidol lowers the seizure threshold and should not be used as a primary agent in this setting.

questions.question: Complete the clinical reasoning bow-tie for this patient. Identify the most likely condition, select TWO priority nursing actions, and identify TWO parameters to monitor. Use the patient scenario to guide your selections.

actions.A: Titrate IV benzodiazepine dose to CIWA-Ar score and administer urgently
actions.B: Administer thiamine 100 mg IV to prevent/treat Wernicke's encephalopathy
actions.C: Implement seizure precautions and create a low-stimulation environment
actions.D: Order a CT head immediately before administering any medications
actions.E: Apply soft wrist restraints to protect the patient from injury
actions.F: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IM for acute agitation management

parameters.A: CIWA-Ar score every 1–2 hours to guide benzodiazepine dosing
parameters.B: Blood pressure and heart rate to assess autonomic instability
parameters.C: Seizure activity: duration, frequency, and character
parameters.D: Level of consciousness and neurological status
parameters.E: Respiratory rate and oxygen saturation (risk of benzodiazepine-induced respiratory depression)

questions.rationale: CONDITION — Delirium tremens (A): The clinical picture is classic for DTs — occurring 24–72+ hours after last drink, with agitation, diaphoresis, tremors, autonomic hyperactivity (HR 132, BP 168/104), visual hallucinations, and a CIWA-Ar of 28. A seizure occurred 20 minutes ago. Delirium of another cause (B) is less likely given the strong alcohol history and timeline. Wernicke's encephalopathy (C) classically presents with the triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia — nystagmus is present but the full triad is not confirmed, and thiamine is already being given. Septic encephalopathy (D) is possible but does not explain the CIWA elevation and withdrawal timeline. ACTIONS — (A) IV benzodiazepine titrated to CIWA-Ar is the cornerstone of DT treatment. (B) Thiamine protects against Wernicke's in a thiamine-depleted patient. (C) Seizure precautions and low-stimulation environment are critical safety and comfort measures. Restraints (E) and haloperidol (F) are inappropriate first-line choices. CT head (D) is not the priority before pharmacologic stabilization. PARAMETERS — All five monitoring parameters (A–E) are clinically indicated: CIWA-Ar guides benzo dosing; BP/HR reflects autonomic instability; seizure monitoring is essential post-ictal; neuro checks detect worsening; respiratory monitoring is critical as benzodiazepines can cause respiratory depression, especially in a patient with pneumonia.

questions.question:
Review the following CIWA-Ar score trend for this patient over the first 18 hours of admission. Lorazepam 2 mg IV was administered PRN at hours 0, 6, and 12 only. What does this trend indicate, and what clinical error in management does it reveal?

Hour 0: CIWA-Ar 18 | Lorazepam given
Hour 6: CIWA-Ar 22 | Lorazepam given
Hour 12: CIWA-Ar 26 | Lorazepam given
Hour 18: CIWA-Ar 28 + Tonic-clonic seizure | No lorazepam given (last dose 5 hours ago)

Select the answer that BEST describes the trend and identifies the management error.

choices.A: The CIWA-Ar scores are within expected limits for alcohol withdrawal; the seizure was an unavoidable complication unrelated to medication management
choices.B: The escalating CIWA-Ar scores despite PRN benzodiazepine administration indicate that PRN-only dosing was insufficient; a scheduled or symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol should have been initiated when the CIWA-Ar exceeded 15
choices.C: The trend indicates adequate management; benzodiazepines should only be given when a seizure occurs, not for CIWA-Ar score elevation alone
choices.D: The CIWA-Ar increase is expected and acceptable; the correct next step is to add an antiepileptic drug such as phenytoin to prevent further seizures
choices.E: The trend indicates the patient is developing tolerance to lorazepam; the correct intervention is to switch to a different benzodiazepine class
choices.F: The rising CIWA-Ar scores indicate that the thiamine dose was too low and should be increased to prevent worsening withdrawal symptoms

questions.rationale: B) CORRECT: This is the correct answer. The CIWA-Ar score rose from 18 → 22 → 26 → 28 over 18 hours despite PRN lorazepam doses at hours 0, 6, and 12. The upward trajectory indicates escalating withdrawal that was NOT being adequately controlled. CIWA-Ar ≥15 indicates severe withdrawal requiring aggressive treatment — a scheduled or symptom-triggered protocol (e.g., lorazepam 2–4 mg IV q1–2h with CIWA-Ar reassessment) is the standard of care for severe withdrawal, NOT a fixed PRN q6h order. The 5-hour gap between the last lorazepam dose and the seizure is a direct consequence of undertreatment. A) INCORRECT: Scores are NOT within acceptable limits — 28 is severe. The seizure was not unavoidable; it was a consequence of undertreatment. C) INCORRECT: Waiting for a seizure to treat is never appropriate. Benzodiazepines should be titrated to CIWA-Ar scores proactively. D) INCORRECT: Phenytoin is NOT effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures (unlike epilepsy). Benzodiazepines remain the drug of choice. E) INCORRECT: The issue is not tolerance but inadequate dosing frequency. F) INCORRECT: Thiamine has no role in controlling withdrawal severity — it prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy.

questions.question: The nurse enters the room and finds the patient experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure in bed. The seizure has been ongoing for 45 seconds. Place the following nursing actions in ORDER of priority (1 = highest priority, first action to take).

choices.A: Protect the patient from injury: do NOT restrain limbs, clear the area of hard objects, turn to lateral position if possible to maintain airway
choices.B: Call for help and activate the rapid response system
choices.C: Time the seizure from onset and continuously observe and document seizure activity
choices.D: Apply supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather mask or nasal cannula
choices.E: Prepare IV lorazepam or diazepam for administration if seizure exceeds 5 minutes (status epilepticus threshold)
choices.F: Insert a tongue depressor or oral airway device to prevent the patient from biting their tongue

questions.rationale: PRIORITY ORDER: 1st — A (Protect from injury): The immediate first priority during any seizure is patient safety. Do NOT restrain the patient — this can cause fractures and increases agitation. Clear the environment, pad rails, and turn the patient to the side if possible to protect the airway. 2nd — B (Call for help): Activate rapid response immediately. This patient has severe DTs, a recent prior seizure, and is on a medical-surgical floor. Additional personnel and resources are needed. 3rd — C (Time the seizure): Accurate timing is critical. If the seizure lasts ≥5 minutes, it meets criteria for status epilepticus requiring emergent pharmacologic intervention. Documentation guides treatment decisions. 4th — D (Oxygen): Seizure activity dramatically increases cerebral metabolic demand and risks hypoxia. The patient's SpO2 was already 96% on room air with pneumonia — supplemental oxygen is a priority. 5th — E (Prepare benzodiazepine): IV lorazepam or diazepam should be drawn up and ready to administer if the seizure crosses the 5-minute threshold for status epilepticus. F) INCORRECT and DANGEROUS: NEVER insert anything into the mouth of a seizing patient. This is a common myth — it risks broken teeth, oral laceration, and injury to the nurse. Patients do not swallow their tongue.

questions.question:
Complete the following clinical teaching statement by selecting the correct answer for each blank. A nurse is educating a new graduate nurse about alcohol withdrawal management.

"The nurse knows that thiamine must be administered [BLANK 1] glucose or dextrose-containing IV fluids in patients with alcohol use disorder to prevent [BLANK 2], which is classically characterized by the triad of [BLANK 3]. Benzodiazepines are considered [BLANK 4] treatment for alcohol withdrawal seizures because they [BLANK 5]."

choices.A: BLANK 1 options: (a) before | (b) after | (c) simultaneously with | (d) instead of
choices.B: BLANK 2 options: (a) hepatic encephalopathy | (b) Wernicke's encephalopathy | (c) Korsakoff psychosis | (d) alcohol-related dementia
choices.C: BLANK 3 options: (a) confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia | (b) jaundice, ascites, and spider angiomata | (c) tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia | (d) hallucinations, delusions, and paranoia
choices.D: BLANK 4 options: (a) second-line | (b) adjunct | (c) first-line | (d) experimental
choices.E: BLANK 5 options: (a) block dopamine receptors to reduce psychosis | (b) cross-react with GABA receptors to suppress CNS excitability and raise the seizure threshold | (c) inhibit glutamate release to reduce excitotoxicity | (d) activate opioid receptors to reduce withdrawal pain

correct.BLANK 5: b) cross-react with GABA receptors to suppress CNS excitability and raise the seizure threshold

questions.rationale: BLANK 1 — 'before': In chronic alcohol users, glucose metabolism requires thiamine (vitamin B1). If glucose/dextrose is administered BEFORE thiamine in a thiamine-depleted patient, the sudden glucose load depletes remaining thiamine stores and can precipitate acute Wernicke's encephalopathy. Thiamine must ALWAYS be given first. BLANK 2 — 'Wernicke's encephalopathy': This is the acute, reversible thiamine-deficiency syndrome that can be precipitated by glucose administration. It is a medical emergency. If untreated, it progresses to the irreversible Korsakoff psychosis. BLANK 3 — 'confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia': This is the classic Wernicke's triad (though not all three are always present). Ophthalmoplegia (paralysis of eye muscles) and nystagmus are the most specific findings. Only ~10–16% of patients present with the full triad. BLANK 4 — 'first-line': Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) are universally recognized as the first-line pharmacologic treatment for alcohol withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens. BLANK 5 — 'cross-react with GABA receptors to suppress CNS excitability and raise the seizure threshold': Alcohol withdrawal causes CNS hyperexcitability due to upregulation of NMDA (excitatory) receptors and downregulation of GABA-A (inhibitory) receptors. Benzodiazepines enhance GABA-A receptor activity, restoring inhibitory tone and suppressing the hyperexcitable state.

questions.question: For each nursing intervention or clinical action listed below, indicate whether it is INDICATED or CONTRAINDICATED in the management of this patient with delirium tremens and a post-seizure state.

choices.A: Administer IV lorazepam when CIWA-Ar score exceeds 15
choices.B: Apply physical restraints as the first-line intervention to manage agitation
choices.C: Administer thiamine before any dextrose-containing IV fluids
choices.D: Place the patient in a quiet, low-stimulation environment with dimmed lighting
choices.E: Use haloperidol as the primary pharmacologic treatment for alcohol withdrawal seizures
choices.F: Use a scheduled or symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing protocol rather than PRN-only dosing for severe withdrawal (CIWA-Ar ≥15)

questions.rationale: A) INDICATED: A CIWA-Ar score >15 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal. IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam) are the standard of care and should be administered promptly per protocol to prevent progression to status epilepticus and death. B) CONTRAINDICATED: Physical restraints are not an appropriate first-line intervention for agitation in alcohol withdrawal. Restraints increase metabolic demand, worsen hyperthermia, escalate agitation, and do not treat the underlying withdrawal. They may be used as a last resort only for immediate safety after pharmacologic management has been initiated. C) INDICATED: Thiamine must precede all glucose/dextrose administration to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy in thiamine-depleted patients with chronic alcohol use disorder. This is a cardinal rule of alcohol withdrawal management. D) INDICATED: Environmental modification is a critical non-pharmacologic intervention. Excessive sensory stimulation worsens agitation, hallucinations, and autonomic instability in DTs. A calm, quiet, dimly lit environment is recommended. E) CONTRAINDICATED: Haloperidol (a typical antipsychotic) lowers the seizure threshold and is CONTRAINDICATED as a primary treatment for alcohol withdrawal seizures. It may have a limited role as an adjunct for persistent hallucinations AFTER adequate benzodiazepine treatment, but never as the primary agent. F) INDICATED: For patients with CIWA-Ar ≥15 (severe withdrawal), a scheduled or symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol is strongly preferred over PRN-only dosing. PRN-only dosing, as demonstrated in this case's trend question, led to escalating withdrawal scores and a breakthrough seizure. Scheduled dosing with CIWA-Ar reassessment every 1–2 hours ensures adequate, consistent CNS suppression.

title: Thyroid Storm: Recognition and Emergency Management
scenario: A 38-year-old female with a known history of Graves' disease presents to the emergency department following an appendectomy performed yesterday. Her family reports she stopped taking propylthiouracil (PTU) approximately 3 weeks ago because she "felt better." The surgical stress appears to have precipitated a thyroid crisis. On assessment, the patient is agitated, confused, and diaphoretic. She has vomited twice since arrival. Vital signs: Temperature 40.8°C (105.4°F), Heart Rate 168 bpm with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (afib with RVR) on telemetry, Blood Pressure 158/72 mmHg (wide pulse pressure of 86), Respiratory Rate 28 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Laboratory results reveal TSH <0.01 mIU/L (critically low) and free T4 at a critically elevated level. The Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale score is calculated at 65, indicating thyroid storm is likely. Two large-bore IV lines have been established. A cooling blanket is in place. The physician has been notified and is at bedside. The nurse prepares to implement emergency thyroid storm management protocol.

questions.question: The nurse is preparing to implement thyroid storm management. Select ALL interventions that are indicated for this patient. Select all that apply.

choices.A: Administer propylthiouracil (PTU) or methimazole orally/via NG tube to block new thyroid hormone synthesis
choices.B: Administer potassium iodide (Lugol's solution) at least 1 hour AFTER PTU to block thyroid hormone release
choices.C: Administer IV propranolol to control heart rate and manage atrial fibrillation with RVR
choices.D: Administer aspirin 650 mg for fever management and antipyretic effect
choices.E: Administer IV dexamethasone to block peripheral conversion of T4 to T3
choices.F: Administer levothyroxine to support thyroid function during the crisis

questions.rationale: Thyroid storm management follows a specific sequence: (A) PTU or methimazole is given FIRST to block new hormone synthesis — this is the critical first step. (B) Iodine solution (Lugol's or potassium iodide) is given at least 1 hour AFTER PTU; if given before, iodine becomes substrate for MORE hormone synthesis, worsening the crisis (Wolff-Chaikoff effect cannot protect if new synthesis hasn't been blocked). (C) IV propranolol controls the life-threatening tachycardia and afib with RVR — beta-blockers also reduce peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. (E) IV dexamethasone inhibits peripheral conversion of T4 to the more active T3, reducing thyrotoxic burden. INCORRECT: (D) Aspirin is CONTRAINDICATED — it displaces thyroid hormone from thyroid-binding globulin (TBG), acutely increasing free T4 and free T3 levels; acetaminophen is used instead. (F) Levothyroxine is contraindicated — the patient has excess thyroid hormone; supplementation would worsen the crisis. Cooling measures (blanket already in place) and treatment of the precipitating cause (appendectomy/surgical stress) are also indicated.

questions.question: Complete the clinical bow-tie diagram. Based on the patient's presentation, identify the most likely condition, then select the appropriate nursing actions to take, and the parameters to monitor to evaluate the patient's response.

choices.A: Condition — Thyroid Storm (Thyrotoxic Crisis)
choices.B: Condition — Malignant Hyperthermia
choices.C: Condition — Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)
choices.D: Condition — Severe Sepsis
choices.E: Action — Administer PTU first, then iodine solution 1 hour later; give IV propranolol and IV dexamethasone; apply cooling measures
choices.F: Action — Administer dantrolene IV immediately; discontinue triggering anesthetic agents; apply aggressive cooling
choices.G: Parameter — Monitor heart rate and rhythm (telemetry), temperature trend, neurological/mental status, blood pressure, and TSH/free T4 levels

questions.rationale: BOW-TIE ANALYSIS: CONDITION (A) — Thyroid Storm: The clinical picture — Graves' disease history, PTU non-compliance, surgical precipitant, hyperthermia (40.8°C), extreme tachycardia with afib, wide pulse pressure, agitation/confusion, diaphoresis, and Burch-Wartofsky score of 65 — is classic for thyroid storm. Malignant Hyperthermia (B) occurs with volatile anesthetics/succinylcholine and presents with rigidity and elevated CK. NMS (C) requires antipsychotic exposure and presents with lead-pipe rigidity. Sepsis (D) would show elevated WBC, cultures, and different lab pattern. ACTIONS (E) — The correct management sequence is PTU first (blocks synthesis), then iodine 1 hour later (blocks release), propranolol IV (controls HR/afib and reduces T4→T3 conversion), dexamethasone (blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion), and cooling. PARAMETERS (G) — Key monitoring parameters: HR/cardiac rhythm (afib resolution, rate control), temperature (trending toward normal confirms treatment efficacy), neurological status (confusion/agitation resolving), blood pressure (wide pulse pressure narrowing), and thyroid labs (TSH/free T4 trending toward normal over time).

questions.question:
Four hours after initiating thyroid storm management, the nurse reviews the following trend data. Click to highlight the finding that requires immediate action, or indicate what this trend demonstrates.

TEMPERATURE: 40.8°C → 40.2°C → 39.6°C → 38.9°C
HEART RATE/RHYTHM: 168 bpm (afib with RVR) → 144 bpm (afib) → 118 bpm (afib) → 96 bpm (NSR)
MENTAL STATUS: Confused/agitated → Less agitated → Oriented to person → Oriented to person and place
BP: 158/72 → 152/74 → 146/76 → 138/78

What does this trend indicate, and what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.A: The patient is showing a positive response to thyroid storm treatment; continue current management and monitoring — do NOT discontinue medications
choices.B: The patient's vital signs are normalizing too quickly, indicating rebound hypothyroidism; reduce PTU dose immediately
choices.C: The conversion from afib to NSR is a dangerous arrhythmia requiring immediate defibrillation
choices.D: The gradual temperature decline is insufficient; escalate cooling measures and add aspirin 650 mg
choices.E: The narrowing pulse pressure indicates developing cardiac tamponade; prepare for pericardiocentesis
choices.F: Mental status changes are resolving too slowly; administer haloperidol for persistent agitation

questions.rationale: TREND ANALYSIS — This trend demonstrates a POSITIVE therapeutic response to thyroid storm treatment: Temperature declining steadily (40.8→38.9°C) without overshooting to hypothermia. Heart rate decreasing progressively (168→96 bpm) with conversion from afib with RVR to normal sinus rhythm (NSR) — a critical safety improvement. Mental status improving in parallel with physiologic normalization. Blood pressure normalizing with narrowing of the previously wide pulse pressure (86 mmHg → 60 mmHg), reflecting reduced catecholamine excess. PRIORITY ACTION: Continue current management — PTU, iodine solution, propranolol, dexamethasone, and cooling. It is critical NOT to stop thyroid-blocking medications prematurely; thyroid storm can rebound. The patient will need continued antithyroid therapy and eventual definitive treatment (radioactive iodine or thyroidectomy) for Graves' disease. INCORRECT OPTIONS: (B) There is no rebound hypothyroidism in 4 hours. (C) Conversion from afib to NSR is a DESIRED outcome of rate/rhythm control. (D) Aspirin is contraindicated in thyroid storm. (E) Narrowing pulse pressure is expected improvement, not tamponade. (F) Mental status IS improving — haloperidol not indicated.

questions.question: The covering provider writes the following order: 'Aspirin 650 mg PO every 4 hours as needed for temperature > 38.5°C.' The patient's current temperature is 40.8°C. What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.A: Administer the aspirin 650 mg immediately, as fever control is the highest priority in thyroid storm
choices.B: Hold the aspirin and contact the provider to question/clarify the order, explaining that aspirin is contraindicated in thyroid storm and requesting acetaminophen instead
choices.C: Administer half the dose (325 mg) to minimize adverse effects while still providing antipyresis
choices.D: Apply an additional cooling blanket and defer the aspirin order until the next physician rounds
choices.E: Document the order as received and administer per protocol, as aspirin is a standard antipyretic
choices.F: Contact pharmacy to verify the aspirin order before administration

questions.rationale: PRIORITY ACTION: The nurse must QUESTION THIS ORDER — it represents a patient safety issue requiring immediate clarification before administration. RATIONALE FOR ASPIRIN CONTRAINDICATION: Aspirin (salicylates) displaces thyroid hormone (T4 and T3) from thyroid-binding globulin (TBG) in the bloodstream. By competing for protein-binding sites, aspirin acutely increases FREE T4 and FREE T3 levels. In thyroid storm, where the patient already has critically elevated thyroid hormone activity, this would significantly worsen the thyrotoxic crisis and could precipitate cardiovascular collapse. CORRECT APPROACH: Contact the provider immediately, explain the pharmacological rationale, and request a substitute order for acetaminophen (Tylenol) 650-1000 mg, which safely reduces fever without affecting thyroid hormone binding. This falls within nursing scope of practice — nurses have a professional obligation to question orders that could harm patients. INCORRECT OPTIONS: (A) Administering would cause harm — never acceptable when contraindication is known. (C) There is no safe aspirin dose in thyroid storm — the mechanism is not dose-dependent at therapeutic levels. (D) Deferring without contacting the provider leaves an unsafe order active. (E) This is NOT a standard scenario — the nurse must apply clinical knowledge. (F) Pharmacy verification alone is insufficient; the prescriber must be notified and the order changed.

questions.question:
Complete the following statements about thyroid storm management by selecting the correct option for each blank.

'In thyroid storm, iodine solution (Lugol's solution or potassium iodide) must be given [BLANK 1] propylthiouracil (PTU) because giving iodine first would [BLANK 2]. Aspirin is [BLANK 3] in thyroid storm because it [BLANK 4], which worsens the crisis.'

Select the correct answer for each blank:

choices.A: BLANK 1: at least 1 hour AFTER administering
choices.B: BLANK 1: simultaneously with
choices.C: BLANK 1: 30 minutes BEFORE administering
choices.D: BLANK 2: provide iodine substrate for new thyroid hormone synthesis, dramatically worsening thyrotoxicosis
choices.E: BLANK 2: cause permanent thyroid suppression and hypothyroidism
choices.F: BLANK 2: trigger the Wolff-Chaikoff effect and block all thyroid hormone production
choices.G: BLANK 3: contraindicated
choices.H: BLANK 3: the first-line treatment
choices.I: BLANK 3: used with caution
choices.J: BLANK 4: displaces thyroid hormone from binding proteins (TBG), acutely increasing free T4 and T3 levels
choices.K: BLANK 4: inhibits prostaglandin synthesis and reduces thyroid hormone secretion
choices.L: BLANK 4: crosses the blood-brain barrier and causes central hypothyroidism

questions.rationale: CLOZE ANSWER EXPLANATIONS: BLANK 1 — (A) CORRECT: Iodine must be given AT LEAST 1 HOUR AFTER PTU. PTU must first block thyroid peroxidase (the enzyme that incorporates iodine into thyroid hormone). Only after this blockade is established can iodine safely be given to block hormone RELEASE from the gland. If iodine is given first, the gland uses the excess iodine as substrate to MANUFACTURE more hormone. BLANK 2 — (D) CORRECT: Without prior PTU blockade, iodine loading provides raw material for thyroid hormone synthesis, worsening the crisis. This is the opposite of the intended Wolf-Chaikoff effect (which requires prior synthesis blockade). BLANK 3 — (G) CORRECT: Aspirin is CONTRAINDICATED — not merely cautioned. This is an absolute contraindication in thyroid storm. BLANK 4 — (J) CORRECT: Salicylates compete with T4 and T3 for binding sites on thyroid-binding globulin (TBG) and other plasma proteins. By displacing protein-bound (inactive) thyroid hormone, aspirin acutely raises FREE hormone levels — the biologically active fraction. In thyroid storm, any increase in free thyroid hormone can be life-threatening. Acetaminophen does NOT have this binding displacement effect and is the safe alternative.

questions.question: For each intervention listed, indicate whether it is Indicated or Contraindicated in the management of this patient with thyroid storm.

choices.A: PTU administered before iodine solution — Indicated
choices.B: PTU administered before iodine solution — Contraindicated
choices.C: Aspirin 650 mg for fever control — Indicated
choices.D: Aspirin 650 mg for fever control — Contraindicated
choices.E: IV propranolol for heart rate control — Indicated
choices.F: IV propranolol for heart rate control — Contraindicated
choices.G: Levothyroxine (T4) supplementation — Indicated
choices.H: Levothyroxine (T4) supplementation — Contraindicated
choices.I: IV dexamethasone — Indicated

matrix_rows.rationale: PTU must ALWAYS precede iodine. PTU blocks thyroid peroxidase, preventing new hormone synthesis. Only then can iodine safely block hormone RELEASE. Reversing the order provides iodine substrate before synthesis is blocked, worsening the crisis.

Aspirin displaces T4 and T3 from thyroid-binding globulin (TBG), acutely increasing free (active) thyroid hormone levels. In thyroid storm, this is life-threatening. Use acetaminophen for fever control instead.

matrix_rows.intervention: IV propranolol for heart rate control
